full_response:
In Alice's rest frame, the device blinks every second, so the proper time between blinks is \(\Delta \tau = 1 \, \text{s}\). Bob observes Alice moving away at \(v = 0.8c\). The Lorentz factor is  
\[
\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.64}} = \frac{1}{0.6} = \frac{5}{3}.
\]  
In Bob's frame, the time between blinks is time-dilated:  
\[
\Delta t = \gamma \Delta \tau = \frac{5}{3} \, \text{s}.
\]  
During this time, Alice travels a distance  
\[
\Delta x = v \Delta t = (0.8c) \left( \frac{5}{3} \, \text{s} \right) = \frac{4}{3} c \, \text{s} = \frac{4}{3} \, \text{light-seconds}.
\]  
This result is also confirmed by Lorentz transformations, where \(\Delta x' = 0\) in Alice's frame leads to \(\Delta x = v \gamma \Delta t'\).

\boxed{\dfrac{4}{3} \ \text{light-seconds}}
Confidence: 100%
